Title: Incorporating Virtual Reality In Undergraduate Internal Medicine Education: A Scoping Review Of Current Evidence And Implementation Strategies
Author: Nagdy, Hanaa Mahmoud1; Massoud, Farah Tamer2; Moheyeldin, Seifeldin Ata2; Basha, Mahmoud Yehia2; Hassan, Saleh Mohamed2; El-Kassas, Mohamed31 Internal Medicine Department, Colleg e of Medicine, Arab Academy for Science, Technology and Maritime Transport, Alexandria, Egypt2 College of Medicine, Arab Academy for Science, Technology and Maritime Transport, Alexandria, Egypt3 Endemic Medicine Department, Faculty of Medicine, Helwan University, Ain Helwan, Cairo, 11795, Egypt m_elkassas@hq.helwan.edu.eg
Abstract: Virtual reality (VR) is increasingly recognized as a valuable tool in undergraduate medical education. It offers immersive, interactive environments that support experiential learning and skill development. However, its specific application in internal medicine education remains variably described. This scoping review aims to explore how VR is currently implemented in undergraduate internal medicine education, identify its educational outcomes, and highlight associated advantages, limitations, and gaps in literature. BACKGROUND Virtual reality (VR) is increasingly recognized as a valuable tool in undergraduate medical education. It offers immersive, interactive environments that support experiential learning and skill development. However, its specific application in internal medicine education remains variably described. This scoping review aims to explore how VR is currently implemented in undergraduate internal medicine education, identify its educational outcomes, and highlight associated advantages, limitations, and gaps in literature.Following the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) methodology and PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we conducted a comprehensive search of eight databases, including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase. Studies were included if they addressed the use of VR in internal medicine education among undergraduate medical students. Data were extracted and analyzed thematically. METHODS Following the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) methodology and PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we conducted a comprehensive search of eight databases, including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase. Studies were included if they addressed the use of VR in internal medicine education among undergraduate medical students. Data were extracted and analyzed thematically.Of 1343 records screened, 9 studies published between 2017 and 2024 met the inclusion criteria. Studies originated from diverse countries and employed immersive and non-immersive VR technologies. Applications included teaching clinical reasoning, procedural skills, and emergency management. VR was associated with improved student engagement, satisfaction, and knowledge retention. Key advantages included scalability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. Limitations included technical challenges, limited physical interaction, and high initial costs. Few studies assessed long-term outcomes or included diverse, low-resource settings. RESULTS Of 1343 records screened, 9 studies published between 2017 and 2024 met the inclusion criteria. Studies originated from diverse countries and employed immersive and non-immersive VR technologies. Applications included teaching clinical reasoning, procedural skills, and emergency management. VR was associated with improved student engagement, satisfaction, and knowledge retention. Key advantages included scalability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. Limitations included technical challenges, limited physical interaction, and high initial costs. Few studies assessed long-term outcomes or included diverse, low-resource settings.VR is a promising adjunct to traditional internal medicine education, enhancing learner engagement and skill acquisition. However, its integration requires addressing technical barriers, ensuring faculty training, and expanding research to include diverse educational contexts and long-term effectiveness. CONCLUSION VR is a promising adjunct to traditional internal medicine education, enhancing learner engagement and skill acquisition. However, its integration requires addressing technical barriers, ensuring faculty training, and expanding research to include diverse educational contexts and long-term effectiveness.
Database: ProQuest One Applied & Life Sciences,SciTech Premium Collection,ProQuest One Health & Nursing
challenges: The specific application of VR in internal medicine education remains variably described, and there are gaps in the literature regarding long-term outcomes and diverse, low-resource settings.
findings: VR is a promising adjunct to traditional internal medicine education, enhancing learner engagement and skill acquisition. It is associated with improved student engagement, satisfaction, and knowledge retention. Key advantages include scalability, safety, and cost-effectiveness. Limitations include technical challenges, limited physical interaction, and high initial costs.
